name: Hosts File Duplicate Entry
description: vCenter operations fail intermittently because the VCSA /etc/hosts file has a duplicate entry pointing the vCenter FQDN to the wrong IP
customerScenario: Hi, I'm Sam from the platform team. vCenter is behaving really strangely — operations succeed sometimes and fail other times. We'll try something, it fails with a connection error, retry immediately and it works fine. This has been happening for two days and started after we applied a vCenter patch.
customerFollowUp: The patch process required some manual steps. The engineer who applied it mentioned adding some entries to a configuration file because 'DNS was too slow during the restart sequence'. I don't know exactly what they changed, but it was something to do with hostname resolution.
fixSteps: SSH to the vCenter Server Appliance (VCSA)
Look for duplicate entries for the vCenter FQDN or short hostname
There may be both a loopback entry (127.0.x.x) and a real IP entry for the same hostname, with the wrong one appearing first
Remove any duplicate or incorrect entries — the correct line should be: <vcenter-real-ip>  <vcenter-fqdn>  <short-hostname>
Ensure 127.0.0.1 only maps to 'localhost' and ::1 only maps to 'localhost ipv6-localhost ipv6-loopback'
Save the file and verify: nslookup <vcenter-fqdn> should consistently return the correct IP
hints: Intermittent failures that immediately succeed on retry often indicate that lookups are alternating between two different results — one correct and one incorrect. Since this started after manual edits to a configuration file, check what was changed.

SSH to the VCSA and inspect /etc/hosts with: cat /etc/hosts. The /etc/hosts file takes precedence over DNS. If there are duplicate or incorrect entries for the vCenter hostname, resolution will alternate between them on each query.

Look at every line in /etc/hosts that contains the vCenter FQDN or short hostname. Are there multiple lines? Does any line point the vCenter name to 127.0.0.1 or a wrong IP address? The first matching entry wins — a wrong first entry causes every other lookup to fail.

The /etc/hosts file has a duplicate entry for the vCenter FQDN — one with the real IP and one with a stale or loopback IP, with the incorrect one listed first. This causes roughly half of all internal lookups to resolve to the wrong address.

Edit /etc/hosts (vi /etc/hosts) and remove the incorrect duplicate entry. The correct line should be: <vcenter-real-ip>  <vcenter-fqdn>  <short-name>. The line starting with 127.0.0.1 should only have 'localhost'. Save and test with nslookup — should consistently return the correct IP every time.
author: vSphere Lab Wizard starter library
